name: Ketamine IV/IO
info: ● Preferred if bronchospasm present, evidence now shows it is safe in head injured patients
● Standard dosing 1-2 mg/kg. Some recommendations for lower dosing in hypotensive patients to 0.75 mg/kg
● To decrease risk of respiratory depression and apnea, administer IV bolus doses over >30 to 60 seconds

name: Ketamine IM
info: Can use with Succinylcholine IM if no IV access

name: Midazolam IV/IO
info: Risk of decreased BP, HR, RR

name: Propofol IV/IO
info: Risk of decreased BP, HR, RR

name: Rocuronium IV/IO
info: ● Standard dose is 1-1.2 mg/kg dose preferred for RSI
● Some recommendations for increased dose to 2 mg/kg in hypotensive patients

name: Succinylcholine IV/IO
info: Avoid in renal failure, suspected hyperkalemia

name: Succinylcholine IM
info: Use with Ketamine IM if no IV access
Avoid in renal failure, suspected hyperkalemia

name: Pancuronium IV/IO
info: No longer recommended for use in RSI

name: Sugammadex IV/IO
info: for emergent rocuronium and vecuronium reversal
caution in patients with cardiac disease
following sugammadex use, if immediate neuromuscular blockade is needed, use cisatracurium or atracurium.

name: Sugammadex IV/IO
info: for emergent rocuronium and vecuronium reversal
caution in patients with cardiac disease
following sugammadex use, if immediate neuromuscular blockade is needed, use cisatracurium or atracurium.

following sugammadex use, if immediate neuromuscular blockade is needed, use cisatracurium or atracurium.

name: Fentanyl Infusion
info: ● Concentrations very based on each pharmacy, confirm concentration before initiating any infusions.
● Start at 25 mcg/hr, titrate by 10 mcg/hr up to 300 mcg/hr

name: Hydromorphone Infusion
info: ● Start at 0.25 mg/hr titrate by 0.25-0.5 mg/hr up to 4 mg/hr
● Concentrations very for each pharmacy, confirm concentration before initiating any infusions.

name: Propofol Infusion
info: ● titrate by 5-20 mcg/kg/min Q 5-10 minutes to sedation goal

name: Ketamine Infusion
info: ● 5 mcg/kg/min, titrate by 2.5 mcg/kg/min Q5 min

name: Dexmedetomidine  IV/IO Bolus
info: 100 mcg/mL concentrated solutions requires dilution before administration

name: Dexmedetomidine Infusion
info: usually diluted in solution to a 4 mcg/mL concentration
titrate by 0.2 mcg/kg/hr every 30 mins to sedation goal

name: Vecuronium Infusion
info: ● 0.8-1.7 mcg//kg/min, titrate by 0.1 mcg/kg/min Q15 min per ventilator synchrony or TOF

name: Rocuronium Infusion
info: ● 3-16 mcg//kg/min, titrate by 0.5-1 mcg/kg/min Q60 min per ventilator synchrony or TOF

name: Cisatracurium Infusion
info: ● 1 to 3 mcg/kg/minute (or 0.06 to 0.18 mg/kg/hour); adjust rate accordingly

name: Cisatracurium Infusion
info: ● 1 to 3 mcg/kg/minute (or 0.06 to 0.18 mg/kg/hour); adjust rate accordingly

name: Tidal Volume
info: ● adjust to plateau pressure < 35 cm H2O

info: ● adjust to plateau pressure < 35 cm H2O

info: ● adjust to plateau pressure < 35 cm H2O

name: Midazolam IV/IM

name: Haloperidol IV/IM
info: ● (not currently FDA approved for IV use)

name: Diphenhydramine IM/IV
info: ● used to prevent or treat acute dystonic reactions associated with anti-psychotic agents

name: Benzotropine IM/IV
info: ● used to prevent or treat acute dystonic reactions associated with anti-psychotic agents

name: Ketamine IM
info: ● 4 mg/kg IM- use ideal or adjusted body weight in obesity. Consider max of 250 or 500mg in extreme agitation.

name: Ketamine IV
info: 0.5-1 mg/kg IV- use ideal or adjusted body weight in obesity. Consider max of 50 or 100mg in extreme agitation
● To decrease risk of respiratory depression and apnea, administer IV bolus doses over >30 to 60 seconds

name: Ketamine IV
info: 0.5-1 mg/kg IV- use ideal or adjusted body weight in obesity. Consider max of 50 or 100mg in extreme agitation
● To decrease risk of respiratory depression and apnea, administer IV bolus doses over >30 to 60 seconds

name: Olanzapine IV/IM
info: IM preferred; if given IV monitor closely for respiratory depression
IV: 5-10 mg, repeat dose as needed after 20 mins (max dose: 20 mg/day)
IM: 5-10 mg Q2 hours (max of 30 mg/day)

name: Olanzapine PO
info: 5-10 mg PO, repeat dose as needed after 2 hours (max dose: 20 mg/day)

name: Ziprasidone IM
info: 10 mg Q2 hours or 20 mg Q4 hours (max of 40 mg/day)

name: Diazepam IV
info: 5-10 mg IV, repeat every 5-10 minutes as needed

name: Lorazepam IV
info: 2-4 mg IV, repeat every 15-20 minutes as needed

name: Midazolam
info: ● 2-5 mg IV every 5 minutes x 3 if needed
● increase to 4-10 mg IV if CIWA > 8

name: Phenobarbital LOAD IV
info: 5-10 mg/kg IV based on Ideal Body Weight
●  max total dose typically ~ 30 mg/kg Ideal Body Weight

name: Phenobarbital
info: ● 130-260 mg IV, repeat every 15-20 minutes as needed
●  increased risk for intubation with used concurrently with benzodiazepines
●  max total dose typically ~ 30 mg/kg Ideal Body Weight

name: Propofol
info: Only to be used for intubation procedure or for sedation post-intubation

name: Chlordiazepxide
info: 25-100 mg PO if CIWA > 8, can repeat q1 hr as needed

name: Epinephrine IM/SubQ 1:1000 (1 mg/mL)
info: ● Max dose 0.5 mg, may repeat every 5-15 mins

name: Epi-Pen
info: Most brands contain 0.3 mg/dose, some contain 0.5 mg

name: Epinephrine 0.1 mg/mL (1:10,000) IV/IO
info: **Reserve for patients unresponsive to multiple IM injections**
Only consider in impending cardiopulmonary arrest when continuous infusion epinephrine is not immediately available; IV bolus administration may be associated with cardiac arrhythmias.
Initial: 0.05 to 0.1 mg using the 0.1 mg/mL solution (may further dilute in 10 mL of NS for a 0.01 mg/mL (10 mcg/mL) concentration); may repeat every 3 minutes

name: Epinephrine Infusion IV/IO
info: ● 0.01-0.2 mcg/kg/min, titrate every 2 to 3 minutes by 0.05 mcg/kg/minute

name: Diphenhydramine PO
info: ●  25 mg Q4-6 hours PRN or 50 mg Q6 hours PRN

name: Prednisone PO
info: ● 20-60 mg PO Daily, followed by taper over 5-7 days

name: 4 Factor PCC
info: ● 25-50 units/kg (max 2500 units) for treatment of intracranial hemorrhage
● 50 units/kg (max 5000 units) for other life-threatening hemorrhage)
● Consider rounding dose to whole nearest whole vial

name: 4 Factor PCC
info: ● 25-50 units/kg (max 2500 units) for treatment of intracranial hemorrhage

name: Droperidol IV/IO
info: ● Do not use in patients with known or suspected QT prolongation (QTc >440 msec in males or >450 msec in females) or congenital long QT syndrome
● Use extreme caution in patients >65 years, those with cardiac disease, bradycardia, electrolyte abnormalities, or on medications that prolong QT interval

name: Metoclopromide PO
info: 10 mg; may repeat after 4-6 hours if need
comes as 1 mg/mL solution, or 5 and 10 mg tabs, max dose 10 mg

name: Ondansetron ODT PO
info: oral dissolving tablet, comes as 4 mg and 8 mg tabs, max dose 8 mg

name: Ondansetron IV/IM
info: 4-8 mg IV or 0.15 mg/kg IV Q8-12 hours

name: Ondansetron IV/IM
info: 4-8 mg IV or 0.15 mg/kg IV Q8-12 hours

name: Prochlorperazine IV/IO
info: ● Hypotension is a possibility with IV administration
● Elderly patients should receive dosages in the lower range and be observed closely, as they are more susceptible to hypotension and neuromuscular reactions

name: Promethazine  IM/IV
info: IV/IM is generally avoided due to high risk of tissue injury.
●  If used, promethazine should be administered diluted and infused over 10-15 minutes or at a rate not to exceed 25 mg/min.
● Adminster through a large bore vein (not hand or wrist) or via a running IV line at port farthest from patient's vein to reduce risk of phlebitis.

name: Promethazine PO
info: This is also the rectal dose. PO also available in 12.5,25, and 50 mg tabs. Max dose 25 mg

name: Acyclovir  IV/IO
info: ● HSV (encephalitis or meningitis), herpes zoster (disseminated, opthalmicus), severe varicella, varicella zoster (retinal necrosis, enephalitis)

● One unit increased hemoglobin by 0.6 to 1.6 g/dL
